cupping_notes: Velvety creamy body, bright acidity, and flavors of milk chocolate, citrus, caramel, and green apple.
description: Romelia SHB EP comes from the cloudy region of the Palmichal rainforest. Bordering the historic and well-known coffee zone of Tarrazu, Mauel Rojas Arias pioneered coffee growing in the Palmichal region. Many farmers observed the success of Don Manuel had with coffee and converted their own sugar plantations into coffee farms. After Don Manuel's death, he left his farm to his sister, Romelia. Thanks to her strength and perseverance, Dona Romeila was able to sustain the quality of her farm. With this, the Beneficio Palmichal was founded in 1933, opening and revolutionizing the area for years to come.

name: Dominican Republic: Organic Ramirez Red Honey, Jarabacoa
Dominican Republic: Organic Ramirez Washed, Jarabacoa
cupping_notes: Smooth creamy body. Sweet undertones, butter, cream, and nuts.
description: Ramirez Estate Bio: In the Dominican Republic, the family-owned Ramirez Estate run by the third generation employs over 400 workers who live and work on the property and make nearly 300% higher wages than Fair Trade Standard. Before the Ramirez family purchased the land in the early 1990s, the land was impotent and bare––natural water springs had long since dried up and farmers had only been raising cattle. Today, the Ramirez Estate is one of the most environmentally-progressive and socially-conscious proprietorships in the world because they recycle materials used during coffee bean processing. First, coffee cherry parts that would normally be thrown out are converted into natural gas through fermentation––in turn, this natural gas powers their facility. Next, the used water is also recycled, filtered, and returned to water systems. Lastly, cherry pulps are added to a compost pile where they’re used as fertilizer useful to nearby farms. In addition to creating exceptional coffee, the Ramirez Estate also gives back to its community in countless ways. By building schools in rural areas, local children no longer have to walk nearly twenty miles a day, and after donating books and computers, students have more opportunities to learn. The Ramirez Estate also allows other farmers in the Dominican Republic to use their processing plant to assist in coffee export.  The pictures seen on our website are part of our personal experience touring the estate and meeting the Ramirez family––they are humble, honest, and produce some of the highest-scoring coffees we carry. Washed: This complex washed-processed coffee is dried with unique rotating dryers, and it comes from the certified Ramirez Estate in the Jarabacoa region of the Dominican Republic. It’s grown at 1,400 meters above sea level with an arabica variety of caturra. Within its process, the Ramirez Washed bean holds the inner workings of the cherry in highest regard––the layers of fruit are stripped, and cherries are almost immediately de-pulped through drying to give the coffee a clean, robust flavor––there is little to no fermentation. Cupping notes: Smooth creamy body. Sweet undertones, butter, cream, and nuts. The finish turns to a sweet vanilla soy milk flavor. Exactly what you expect from an island coffee. Learn more about Dominican coffee beans with our DR coffee guide. Try before you stock up with a 1/2 pound sample!

name: Dominican Republic: Ramirez Natural, Jarabacoa
cupping_notes: Bright fruity notes of strawberry, cherry and cream.
description: Ramirez Estate Bio: In the Dominican Republic, the family-owned Ramirez Estate run by the third generation employs over 400 workers who live and work on the property and make nearly 300% higher wages than Fair Trade Standard. Before the Ramirez family purchased the land in the early 1990s, the land was impotent and bare––natural water springs had long since dried up and farmers had only been raising cattle. Today, the Ramirez Estate is one of the most environmentally-progressive and socially-conscious proprietorships in the world because they recycle materials used during coffee bean processing. First, coffee cherry parts that would normally be thrown out are converted into natural gas through fermentation––in turn, this natural gas powers their facility. Next, the used water is also recycled, filtered, and returned to water systems. Lastly, cherry pulps are added to a compost pile where they’re used as fertilizer useful to nearby farms. In addition to creating exceptional coffee, the Ramirez Estate also gives back to its community in countless ways. By building schools in rural areas, local children no longer have to walk nearly twenty miles a day, and after donating books and computers, students have more opportunities to learn. The Ramirez Estate also allows other farmers in the Dominican Republic to use their processing plant to assist in coffee export. The pictures seen on our website are part of our personal experience touring the estate and meeting the Ramirez family––they are humble, honest, and produce some of the highest-scoring coffees we carry. Natural: This Ramirez Estate bean is naturally-processed coffee grown in the Jarabacoa region of the Dominican Republic (which is 1,400 meters above sea level). Its fruit is left on the bean without disturbance for weeks on end, allowing ample time for fermentation. Once separated at a mill, the beans are dried with rotating dryers. The notes are fruity, but not overwhelming. Cupping notes: Smooth and low in acid, with a creamy body. Aromas and tasting notes of almonds, walnuts, and chocolate. Learn more about Dominican coffee beans with our DR coffee guide. Try before you stock up with a 1/2 pound sample!

name: Ethiopia: Konga G2, Yirgacheffe
cupping_notes: Apricot, Floral, Raspberry
description: The Konga Cooperative, one of many in the area, belongs to a much larger group known as the Yirgacheffe Coffee Farmers Union (YCFCU). Ethiopian heirloom coffee bean varietals are chosen from the forest and then transplanted to small gardens, adding to the incredible complex flavor profiles available among wild coffee plants. Konga is an area within Yirgacheffe known for producing particularly fine coffees. We think this lot is no exception. As part of the cooperative's mission to give back to its community, it recently renovated buildings at a primary school and outfitted it with new supplies. Cupping Notes: Apricot, Floral, Raspberry.

name: Ethiopia: Longberry, Harrar
cupping_notes: Smooth, full body with a winey, sweet, and bright acidity and fruity notes of blueberry.
description: Harrar is known as one of the main coffee-growing regions in Ethiopia’s Eastern Highlands, and it produces a very distinctive wild-varietal Arabica. As the name implies, Ethiopian Harrar coffee beans bring the sought-after flavors of blueberry, and it’s naturally processed in order to bring out the most complimenting flavor possible. There’s a unique and subtle “peppering” that occurs during processing––the color of the beans disperses. The Longberry’s name refers to the Ethiopian grading term used to describe the largest beans, so there isn’t any abnormality in the actual length. Its namesake, the walled city of Harrar has a population of 200,000, and is part of a key trade route. Harrar is not only the eastern-most growing region in the whole country, but it’s also a remarkable coffee with a deeper, less fruity taste than our Misty Valley bean. Cupping Notes:Smooth, full body with a winey, sweet, and bright acidity and fruity notes of blueberry. Try before you stock up with a 1/2 pound sample!

name: Guatemala: Decaf HHT Royal Select, Huehuetenango
Guatemala: Dulce Leonarda Estate HHT, Huehuetenango
cupping_notes: Great aroma, very clean, smooth mouthfeel, a nice syrupy-sweetness, medium acidity, great body and notes of caramel, cocoa, tobacco and lemony-citrus with some floral hints on a pleasant, lingering finish.
description:
The Guatemala Huehuetenango Dulce Leonarda Estate’s farmlands cover roughly 590 acres
and vary in elevation from 5,000 to 5,400 feet, making this a true SHB grade (Strictly Hard Bean)
Guatemala arabica. The family produces an assortment of 100% shade grown Bourbon, Catuai
and Caturra varietals, handpicks their cherries and operates their own wet mill right at the farm.

name: Guatemala: Organic Asdecafe SHB, Huehuetenango
cupping_notes: Tangerine, creamy, golden raisin, toffee.
description: Grown under guava, plantain, and banana trees in Guatemala’s Huehuetenango region, La Asociación Sostenible de Cafe de Guatemala (ASDECAFE) proudly produces a bean committed to sustainability, environmental protection, equitable distribution among its workers, and of course, the best coffee quality imaginable available internationally. With more than 1,000 active members today, ASDECAFE was founded in 2103 by only six farmers and continues to grow while becoming certified organic. Cupping Notes: Tangerine, creamy, golden raisin, toffee. Try before you stock up with a 1/2 pound sample!

name: Honduras: Kingdom Growers SHG, Montecillos
cupping_notes: Well balanced, sweet citrus fragrance, good acidity with sweet orange flavor, molasses, and a creamy body and finish.
description: A breathtaking Honduran coffee that has won and placed top three for America’s Best Cold Brew Competition, Honduras Kingdom Growers can be found on the Montecillos Mountain Range’s extraordinary slopes, where a cooperative of small coffee growers are dedicated to producing high-quality, sustainable coffee as a community. Each year, the Honduras Kingdom Growers cooperative works with nearly 200 small farm holders and provides them with access to an expanding eco-friendly knowledge base––this gives the farm holders a variety of training and farm-management resources, including processing and milling techniques to ensure a fantastic bean is produced. Honduras Kingdom Growers farmers use teamwork to produce more yields while simultaneously raising income for the people’s benefit. In Honduras, high-grade, specialty coffee is an ever-growing need. Thus, the Honduran farms range from one to twelve acres in size and spread between elevations of 4,000 to 5,2000 feet. Bean lots are hand-picked and processed together to ensure the highest quality cup. La Paz, Comayagua, Santa Barbara, and Intibucá are locales within the Montecillos region––IHCAFE (The Honduran Institute of Coffee) defines the peachy Montecillos as one of the six major coffee-growing regions in Honduras. Cupping Notes: Well balanced, sweet citrus fragrance, good acidity with sweet orange flavor, molasses, and a creamy body and finish.

name: Honduras: Organic 18 Rabbit Black, Marcala
cupping_notes: Black tea and honey notes with a mild complementing lemongrass acidity. Clean, sweet, balanced, and floral.
description: The term “Honey Process” refers to how sticky the coffee beans get while processed––it marks the halfway point between a washed coffee and a naturally-processed coffee. The product has a mellow, fruity taste, and the more mucilage left on the bean, the sweeter it becomes. These subcategories of the amount of mucilage and drying time are divided into yellow, red, golden, black, and white honey beans. This black honey flavor is achieved by fermenting the coffee’s cherries for up to 24 hours––the heaviest period–– before de-pulping and drying on raised beds. 18 Rabbit is female-owned and won the Honduras Cup of Excellence in 2014––Señora Flhor’s coffee has been auctioned for over $35 a pound. The 13 farms are separated into micro-lots and overseen by other members of the Zelaya Onteras family, and they’re paid over 300% higher wages than Fair Trade and a high score of 87 points for their noteworthy organic coffee. Cupping Notes: Black tea and honey notes with a mild complementing lemongrass acidity. Clean, Sweet, Balanced, and Floral.Try before you stock up with a 1/2 pound sample!

name: Honduras: Organic 18 Rabbit Natural, Marcala
cupping_notes: Strawberry aromas that carry over into the cup with fruit forward flavors, hints of chocolate, and a thin body-well balanced.
description: Señora Flhor refers to the 18 Rabbit Natural as her “crunchberry” coffee due to the sweet flavor it develops after natural processing––a back-to-basics approach stemming from Ethiopia also known as “dry processing.” In natural processing, the fruit is left on the bean and the coffee is barely disrupted while drying. This process differs from washed coffee because naturally processed beans are sun-dried with the cherry intact. The 18 Rabbit Naturals are grown on their namesake farmland family-owned by Señora Flhor, her mother, and 11 other relatives. 18 Rabbit’s beans are grown on a micro-lot in the Marcala region, and roasts well at medium (crisp) or dark (sweet). Cupping notes: Strawberry aromas that carry over into the cup with fruit forward flavors, hints of chocolate, and a thin body-well balanced.Try before you stock up with a 1/2 pound sample!

name: Honduras: Organic 18 Rabbit Red, Marcala
cupping_notes: Aromas of strawberry, raspberry, and cherry that carry over into the cup and a heavier body with mild fruit notes, graham cracker, and walnuts. Balanced acidity and sweetness, well rounded, smooth, and juicy.
description: The name “Honey Process” actually comes from how sticky the beans get during processing. This type of processing coffee beans is halfway between a washed coffee and a natural process coffee: it’s fruity, but not in as exaggerated a way as some naturals. It often has a more rounded acidity than washed coffees, with intense sweetness and complex mouthfeel. Typically, the more mucilage left on the bean, the sweeter the taste. Subcategories have developed based on the amount of mucilage that is left as well as their drying times: yellow, red, golden, black, and white honey. 18 Rabbit is a collective of farms in Honduras that are female owned by Senora Flhor, her mother, and different members of the Zelaya Ontreras family. Dulce Marlen, the owner of Finca Dulce Luna, is the matriarch of the family, and owner of 18 Rabbit exports. She and her husband paved the way for organic and biodynamic farming practices, being intentional about naturally regenerating the soil they use for their agriculture. Members of this family have placed in the top 10 for Honduras' Cup of Excellence, and work together to create these unique fermentations you'll find in these coffees. The Red Honey Process is fermented for 24 hours, and mostly at night in the cool weather. The cooler weather creates a slower ferment that you can taste in this coffee. Cupping Notes: Aromas of strawberry, raspberry, and cherry that carry over into the cup with a heavier body and mild fruit notes of graham cracker, and walnuts. Balanced acidity and sweetness, well rounded, smooth, and juicy.

name: Honduras: Organic 18 Rabbit Washed, Marcala
cupping_notes: Chocolate flavors and a mild green apple-like acidity with a heavy body and very sweet.
description: 18 Rabbit––part of Honduras’ Cup of Excellence––is a collection of farms owned by Señora Flhor, her mother, and eleven members of their family. As pioneers of organic and biodynamic coffee-farming techniques, the soil they use is always naturally regenerated. 18 Rabbit Washed coffees are grown in the Marcala region and wash-processed, which gives the roast a sweet flavor.  Cupping Notes: Chocolate and caramel aromas with chocolate flavors and a mild green apple-like acidity with a heavy body and very sweet.Try before you stock up with a 1/2 pound sample!

name: Honduras: Organic 18 Rabbit Yellow, Marcala
cupping_notes: Mild floral notes that carry over into the flavor, very sweet, creamy body with honeysuckle flavors.
description: “Honey Process” indicates how sticky the coffee beans can get during processing, and it yields a sweet, fruity taste depending on how long it’s developed based on the amount of mucilage and drying times. It has a complex mouthfeel halfway between a washed coffee and a naturally-processed coffee. Honey processing is divided into subdivisions: yellow, red, golden, black, and white honey. Female-owned farmland, 18 Rabbit paved the way for organic and biodynamic farming practices––the farmers regenerate the soil used for their agriculture. It’s because of their unique fermentation and dedication to the environment that they placed top 10 for Honduras’ Cup of Excellence. The 18 Rabbit Yellow Honey Process is sometimes referred to as semi-washed––it’s fermented for less than eight hours––when de-pulped, the beans are strained and put out to dry. Cupping Notes: Mild floral notes that carry over into the flavor, very sweet, creamy body with honeysuckle flavors. Try before you stock up with a 1/2lb sample!!

name: Indonesia: Organic Decaf Bali Hai, Indonesia Blend
cupping_notes: Muted earthiness with flavors of cocoa and marshmallow.
description: Bali Hai Organic Swiss Water Process Decaf is becoming one of our most popular coffees, and for a reason: it's good!  A delicate blend of Indonesian coffees, decaffeinated using the Swiss Water Process. A very mellow, yet full-tasting blend; imagine a well-played Baritone Saxophone - now drink it. There are muted, earthy, cocoa and marshmallow tones. This is a great, quiet evening, after-dinner drink, even the name conjures up romance, but you'll have to supply your own caffeine.Cupping Notes: Very clean with flavors of cocoa and marshmallow. Try before you stock up with a 1/2 pound sample!

name: Kenya: Chepkube AB, Bungoma County
cupping_notes: Citrus ,lemon, grapefruit, black tea
description:
This Kenyan coffee comes from the Chepkube Factory in Mt Elgon, Western Kenya, situated at 1580 meters above sea level, on the Kenya-Uganda border. The co-op has approximately 3600 active small-scale farm members who cultivate on less than 1.5 hectare farms that sit on the slopes of Mt Elgon.


Local farmers handpick only fully ripe cherries before delivering them to Chepkube Factory for processing. Upon arrival, cherries undergo careful hand-sorting and weighing to ensure quality standards. The traditional Kenyan washed process includes pulping followed by 24-hour fermentation in tanks, allowing the coffee's characteristic bright acidity to develop.


After fermentation, the parchment coffee is sun-dried on raised beds for 14-21 days, with regular turning to ensure even drying. A final round of meticulous hand-sorting removes any remaining defects before the coffee is prepared for export, ensuring the clean, vibrant cup quality that Nyeri coffees are renowned for.
Cupping Notes: Citrus ,lemon, grapefruit, black tea notes; bright crisp acidity; vibrant fruitiness with a sweet finish. Strong brown sugar aroma with a good delivery.

name: Kenya: Select Plus AA, Kiambu County
cupping_notes: Clean well balanced intense flavor, smooth medium body, sweet lingering aftertaste, bright crisp acidity and sweet brown sugar aroma with  blueberry, raspberry and black tea notes.
description: Our Kenya AA Select Plus is produced by a number of small farmers in the central Kenyan farmlands that surround Mt. Kenya. It is a single-origin coffee from the Nairobi Coffee Exchange and is considered some of the best quality coffee in the world! The Kenyan Process is a double fermentation method where the puled cherries are soaked twice in fermentation tanks and are sun-dried on raised beds for 1-2 weeks. The lots are consolidated at a local mill and go through a double fermentation method before being sun-dried on raised beds. The Nairobi Coffee Exchange grades the beans, cups the coffee, and sells it at auction, where we buy it for you.  Cupping Notes: Clean well balanced intense flavor, smooth medium body, sweet lingering aftertaste, bright crisp acidity and sweet brown sugar aroma with  blueberry, raspberry and black tea notes. Try before you stock up with a 1/2 pound sample!

name: Mexico: Organic Cepco Oaxaca, Oaxaca
cupping_notes: Cupping Notes: Smooth, complex, and clean with a rich body, honeyed, sweet fruit aroma that hints at a juicy ripe fruit & citrus flavor profile with pleasing cocoa undertones throughout.
description: Hailing from the southwestern Mexican state of Oaxaca to the western mountain ranges of Chiapas, this bean has a rich cultural history––since 1989, the Oaxaca State Coffee Producers Network (CEPCO) has been uniting and supporting producers in order to create the highest quality Mexican Oaxaca coffee bean with no stale flavors. The funds from CEPCO’s efforts went on to promote women’s organizations, provide support for families, and finance coffee production infrastructure improvements. Today, 90% of CEPCO’s farmers own less than 2 hectares to ensure a smooth harvest. Cupping Notes: Cupping Notes: Rich body with aroma of honeyed & sweet fruit that hints at a juicy ripe fruit & citrus flavor profile with pleasing cocoa undertones.  Try before you stock up with a 1/2 pound sample!

name: Mexico: Rancho San Francisco, Chiapas
cupping_notes: Medium body, medium acidity, smoky with almond nuttiness and slight hints of peach.
description: Rancho San Francisco is an exclusive to our trading partner, Vournas, and perhaps one of the most underrated and undervalued coffees in all Central America. The “Rancho” as we call it is Strictly High Grown (SHG) by third generation farmers, Delmar and Fernando Moreno Guillen on their family estate in the mountains of Chiapas. The farm utilizes a significant amount of natural shade trees, and while not certified organic, it is naturally grown and processed without chemical fertilizers. Our Mexico Rancho San Francisco is comprised of mostly Typica and Bourbon varieties, but there are a few Caturra trees still producing in some areas of the farm. Overall this is one of the most versatile coffees around—it can be roasted across the spectrum, blended, or enjoyed as solo cup or single origin espresso. The beans are relatively softer than other high grown centrals and develop a bit faster in the roast, coming out looking nice and BIG! Cupping Notes: Medium body, medium acidity, with notes of vanilla, almond nuttiness and slight hints of peach. Try before you stock up with a 1/2 pound sample!

name: Panama: Finca La Santa SHB EP, Boquete
cupping_notes: This well-balanced coffee opens with sweet vanilla, brown sugar, and honey aromas. The smooth, medium body carries vibrant acidity with notes of peach, citrus, and dark chocolate, culminating in a sweet floral finish.
description:
When you think of some of the best coffee in the world, you think of Panama––specifically, the Geisha variety. However, Panama’s Boquette wash-processed beans should not be discounted, as they’re also grown in Boquette Valley’s nutrient-rich volcanic soil. Every step from the first sprout to patio drying is done at 1,200 to 1,400 meters altitude. Taste and find out why Panama is affectionately called “The Bean Belt!”
 

Cupping Notes:This well-balanced coffee opens with sweet vanilla, brown sugar, and honey aromas. The smooth, medium body carries vibrant acidity with notes of peach, citrus, and dark chocolate, culminating in a sweet floral finish.


Try before you stock up with a 1/2 pound sample!

name: Papua New Guinea: Arokara AA, Eastern Highlands
cupping_notes: From the Arokara Cooperative in the East Highlands of Papua New Guinea this green coffee bean has notes of dark chocolate and plum with wine like acidity.
description: Papua New Guinea coffees are known for their complex acidity and bountiful variety due to cultural and topographical diversity––few plantations are large, and most happen to be small plots. Processing methods are constantly being updated to ensure the best coffee year after year. This specific Arakara AA hails from the Arokara Cooperative, a partner of plantations in the Eastern Highlands of Papua, New Guinea. Recently, the plantations have been transferred to the original owners who believe in a chemical-free production process. The coffee’s cherry is hand-picked by the clan, immediately pulped, and fermented in cement vats for three days. Then, the coffee is washed with the Aru River’s water and sun-dried for seven to twelve days. The plantations hire up to 60 assistants to the clan cherry pickers––nearly 12,000 people depend on this process for income to support their families. Cupping notes: Fruit and sweet floral aroma, dark chocolate, plum, earthy; creamy body, winy acidity. Try before you stock up with a 1/2lb sample!! Learn more about PNG coffee beans with our dedicated guide.

name: Papua New Guinea: Organic Purosa A/X, Eastern Highlands
cupping_notes:
Sweet raw sugar aroma, good acidity and creamy body, caramel overtones with hints of dried sweet apricot and a slight cocoa note on the
finish.
description:
Our Fair-Trade Organic (certified by the Highland Organic Agricultural Cooperative) Papua New Guinea Purosa AX hails from the Eastern Highlands provinces––Ivangoi, Purosa, and Okapa––and is procured by over 3,000 farmers. Due to the mineral-rich volcanic soil and jungle climate, Papua New Guinea farms have some of the best growing conditions! The majority of farmers here grow their varietals in small gardens within their tribal villages, which have for centuries largely kept intact their individual languages and customs. Some of the trees which produce our Fair Trade Organic Papua New Guinea are 25+ years old, producing less fruited cherries, but also a higher sugar content than younger crops, which of course translates to a sweeter and more complex cup.This lot is de-pulped and dried in parchment––it’s then loaded onto trucks as it makes its way to Goroka, where it’s milled, graded, and bagged. Once the coffee is ready for export, it’s taken to the Port of Lae––the country’s largest, most successful port. 

Cupping Notes: Sweet raw sugar aroma, good acidity and creamy body, caramel overtones with hints of dried
sweet apricot and a slight cocoa note on the finish Try before you stock up with a 1/2 pound sample! Learn more about PNG coffee beans with our helpful guide!

name: Rwanda: Dukunde Kawa Ruli, Gakenke Districk
Seasoning Beans
cupping_notes: Seasoning Beans
description:
Need some beans to season your roaster? 

Try this blend of beans!

We all know what it's like to buy a roaster and destroy the first few batches while you're getting into the swing of things! Roast a few batches to a dark/vienna roast, and you'll be priming your roaster with a nice seasoning to build upon!

What's in this blend? It could be anything! Organics, Conventionals, Honey Processes, Peaberries - you name it, it's probably in here. Which means that if you *do* happen to use these for drinking instead of just seasoning and experimentation, you're looking at an adventure in every cup!

(We won't guarantee that it'll be the most well rounded cup you've ever had - but it beats instant, and it's probably better than what you had last week at Sunday school or at that town hall meeting!)

*Note: We typically suggest around 3-5 roasts to get your roasting drum seasoned, so keep that in mind when you're choosing how many seasoning beans to order!*

Try before you stock up with a 3lb or 5lb sample!!

name: Sulawesi: Kalossi, Tana Toraja
cupping_notes: Well-balanced delivering intense flavor with a smooth, creamy medium-to-full body. Sweet brown sugar and honey aromas introduce mild, crisp acidity complemented by notes of citrus, orange, nectarine, cherries, brown spice, and dark chocolate. Finishes with a clean, sweet, lingering aftertaste.
description:
From Sulawesi, the Indonesian island previously known as Celebes, "Kalossi" is the former Dutch word for the island. It predates "Toraja" as a name for this coffee. The producers in this region have many acceptable conditions for growing coffee like volcanic soil and thick forests for shade that maintain moisture needed for the coffee plants. Small family farms grow and process their own coffee cherries and use the wet hulled process method that leaves the beans exposed while they dry on patios then they are collected and transported to a central dry mill for final preparation, sorting, and exporting.
 

The Kalossi is an outstanding cup for those who like a full-bodied, aromatic, wild and spicy, highly-complex brew, and a little funkier than the Toraja. Compared to the Toraja, the Kalossi picks up some additional earthy character from the additional chaff.
 

Cupping Notes: Well-balanced delivering intense flavor with a smooth, creamy medium-to-full body. Sweet brown sugar and honey aromas introduce mild, crisp acidity complemented by notes of citrus, orange, nectarine, cherries, brown spice, and dark chocolate. Finishes with a clean, sweet, lingering aftertaste.
 
Try before you stock up with a 1/2 pound sample!

name: Sumatra: Raja Batak Peaberry, Lintong
harvest: Main Crop: October/December; Fly Crop: March/April
cupping_notes: A brisk acidity, heavy body, and sweet and clean with notes of pipe tobacco and cedar and a long, rich finish.
description:
Sumatra Lintong Raja Batak: A Premium Coffee Selection


The Sumatra Lintong Raja Batak coffee originates from select producers near Lintongnihuta in Northern Sumatra Province. This region sits on the southern shores of Lake Toba, the world's largest volcanic lake. The volcanic soil creates exceptional coffee-growing conditions. The Lintong Nihuta plateau ranges from 1,000-1,300 meters in elevation.


Small-scale farmers with 2-4 hectare plantations (roughly 1000 trees per hectare) produce this exceptional coffee. Each farmer handles growing, harvesting, and initial processing. After de-pulping, the coffee ferments overnight and is washed the next day. Farmers briefly dry the parchment to remove surface moisture before delivering it to the production facility.


Upon arrival, each batch undergoes a rigorous inspection. Only coffee meeting the highest standards is purchased and placed in greenhouses for drying to approximately 40% moisture. After hulling, the coffee returns to greenhouses for further drying until reaching 12-13% moisture content.


The coffee then travels to a warehouse for another round of inspection and grading, followed by a two-month resting period. Final processing includes selecting out the peaberries, hand-sorting, and preparing for export.


 Raja Batak is a premium Peaberry selection and is very limited in its availability.
 

Cupping Notes: A brisk acidity, heavy body, and sweet and clean with notes of pipe tobacco and cedar and a long, rich finish.
 

Try before you stock up with a 1/2 pound sample!

name: Sumatra: Tiger, Lintong
cupping_notes: A nice, earthy aroma, creamy, big body, and notes of dark chocolate, honey, and black pepper. Substantial mouthfeel and a pleasant lingering aftertaste.
description:
This is one of the cleanest Lintongs we've ever tasted! 

Absent the musty, heavy earth flavors typical of Sumatrans, and with pleasant honey and chocolate overtones, this coffee will surely please and surprise long-time Lintong lovers, and invite new admirers!

Cupping Notes: A nice, earthy aroma, creamy, big body, and notes of dark chocolate, honey, and black pepper. Substantial mouthfeel and a pleasant lingering aftertaste.

name: Tanzania Peaberry, Kilimanjaro
cupping_notes: Sweet brown sugar honey aroma, juicy smooth medium to full body, clean sweet fruity lingering aftertaste, exotic well balanced intense flavor and crisp bright tangy winey acidity with black tea, citrus, orange and blueberry notes
description:
This African coffee variety is wild and gamey, the good kind of gamey that tells you this is not a domesticated coffee. This coffee is a blend of various coffee producers from across the Kilimanjaro and Arusha regions. These groups come from districts in Kilimanjaro such as Hai and Moshi Rural district; in addition, the coffee was also sourced from the Meru district close to the town of Arusha. The producers of this coffee have farms between 1-2 hectares in size and intercrop with bananas and avocado. The coffee is fully washed and processed on the farms using locally made hand pulpers and buckets for fermentation and dried on raised beds. 

Cupping Notes: Sweet brown sugar honey aroma, juicy smooth medium to full body, clean, sweet, fruity lingering aftertaste, exotic well, balanced, intense flavor, and crisp, bright, tangy winey acidity with black tea, citrus, orange, and blueberry notes.
 

Try before you stock up with a 1/2 pound sample!

name: Uganda: Kaweri Robusta, Mubende
cupping_notes: This Uganda Kaweri Robusta has the taste of peanut, popcorn, salted caramel, vanilla, slightly floral, pear, malty, quick finish, light body.
description:
This premium Ugandan robusta is grown on the Kaweri farm, which cultivates 1,626 hectares of selected Ugandan coffee under indigenous shade trees. The farm produces high-quality, dense, hard beans comparable to high-grown Arabica and is pioneering the washed process for Robusta in Uganda. Their meticulous harvesting and processing methods include selective cherry picking and a thorough post-harvest process involving pulping, soaking, sun pre-drying, and final drum drying.


Cupping Notes: Peanut, popcorn, salted caramel, vanilla, slightly floral, pear, malty, quick finish, light body.

name: Yemen: Northern Crown Khulani, Sa'adah Governorate
cupping_notes: Cocoa, earthy, light fruit, medium body, Caramelized banana, Dark Chocolate. Fresh, smooth, pleasant herbal notes
description:
Yemen: Northern Crown Khulani, Sa'adah Governorate is cultivated by small legacy farmers within the Khulani Coffee Society for Agricultural Development (KCSAD), a grower coalition spanning across Sa'adah. The coalition focuses on advancing farm investments and quality standards throughout the harvest and post-harvest processes.


Yemen holds the distinction of being Earth's first coffee cultivation territory. Its seeds, originally from Ethiopian arabica landraces, established the world's first commercial coffee farms, so the coffee tradition runs deep. The challenging cultivation environment of Yemen's western and northern ranges demands generations-old farming techniques


These coffee-growing families maintain ancestral terraced lands where coffee remains both an economic lifeline and a binding social tradition.  "Khulani" designates premium heirloom coffee varieties grown in Yemen's northern ranges, processed naturally through careful hand-picking, sorting, and sun-drying on raised beds or rooftops.


Despite Yemen's ongoing civil conflict, the coffee community continues to produce some of the most sought after coffees in the world. Their mission focuses on increasing both quality coffee availability from Yemen's historic growing regions and global market presence.

 
Cupping Notes: Cocoa, earthy, light fruit, medium body, Caramelized banana, Dark Chocolate. Fresh, smooth, pleasant herbal notes.


Try before you stock up with a 1/2 pound sample!
